speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Ruth Minsky Sender. The interview is taking place in Commack, New York. The interviewer is Anne Bernard. Let's pick up on why you feel this is important testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the Holocaust deniers. One of my students-- not my students-- one student that read and he had to do a project on had sent me-- among other letters, he sent a letter telling me that he went to the internet for more information and background on the project he was doing. And on the internet, he said it was pretty scary. Because it said that the Holocaust-- there were some that will say that the Holocaust never happened. And there were others that said that it could have-- have happened, but it was a noble venture. And if it wouldn't be for the Jews brainwashing the world-- I don't remember the exact quotation from his letter-- the world would have realized that Hitler had the right idea. Now, this is scary. And we have to teach that it did happen by bearing witness. We can't let them start spreading hate again. And even if it's small groups-- because the boy said it was only a small group-- we know what happened when Hitler started with a small group. He only had seven members. So I think it's-- it's very important that the survivors should bear witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Morris Sender. And in back of me it's my oldest son, Louis Sender. Next to him is Judy, his wife. And this is Nancy, my daughter. And this is Amy, who just graduated college.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I didn't forget. And my granddaughter Jennifer-- I wouldn't say she's a teacher. [LAUGHTER] She's a teacher. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say a little bit. I-- this is a very difficult thing for anyone to do. It's difficult for my-- my mother. It's difficult for anyone, basically. And I-- and I appreciate the whole [BACKROUND NOISE] concept. It's-- it's impossible to understand what happened during that time. It's impossible if you're a relative. It's impossible if you've grown up with people who survived. It's impossible if you don't know anyone associated with it. It's impossible even for people who are seeing this to understand it. You just don't feel what-- what happened, for a lot of different reasons. And it's-- it's very important that everyone know, because maybe the more stories you get on camera, the more there'll be some reality to it, the more there will be some credibility to it. And I just wanted to let you know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My two other sons, Allen and Harvey-- one lives in Georgia, and one lives in Colorado, [SIGHS] and their families. They could not be here, but they're here in spirit and-- and pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to say how proud I am of my mother, because she's-- how strong she is to do this. She's been doing this for many years. And no matter how hard it is for her, she continues to do it. And I wanted to say that people don't also realize how it affects the families, how-- how you grow up, you know, even just without grandparents, you know, people take things for granted, you know, what they have, what they-- they don't appreciate what they have. And they really need to-- to learn more about it, more of it-- more in terms of people to people, rather than just historical-- you know, a chapter in a history book. So I think that by my mother speaking about it, it makes it more real for them, and they see it as-- as this is-- this is a survivor. This is an actual person. It's not just something they read about and just go on to the next chapter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say how grateful and fortunate I am to have my grandmother and my grandfather here, alive with us, to tell us these stories. And like Nancy said about learning in the history books, I was learning things in books when I was younger and coming home and talking to my grandmother about it firsthand. And-- and the speakers that came into school were-- were my grandmother. And I don't think anybody could have been luckier than that. And I really am very fortunate. And I am aware of that. And we're lucky people. And this is a lucky time in our lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if there's much left to say. I mean, we all know how lucky we are that they're alive with us today to have these stories to tell us. Growing up, it's very different, when you come to Grandma's house and you think of all the things that Grandma does, our grandma was also very different in a lot of ways. A lot of the things that she told us about, and-- and the stories that she had for us were not the same stories that other people got. And I think it also helped not only that she told us the stories, but that we told other people stories, that they weren't stories just from a survivor. They're stories from survivors' families. And I think like Amy said, that growing up, having Grandma come to speak to classes was a wonderful thing. But I think really, it was much more effective-- I mean, the other day, when she came to speak to my own classroom full of kids, who have the same questions that I did, and were learning the same things from her, and were feeling the same emotions. And I think it's a wonderful thing that-- not only that she could talk about it, but that she's here with us today, and that she's willing to talk about it. And I think that's important too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I appreciate all of you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we love you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something, Judy?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKROUND NOISE] All right. I come from a family that was not involved in Europe during the war. And marrying into this family some 27 years ago, and having my first exposure to Holocaust survivors, was a rather difficult experience. At the time, my mother-in-law did not speak of it, although it was something that was in the history and unspoken. I have been witness to the unfolding, and the maturity, and the understanding of the need of telling and retelling the story. I think the oral history project is invaluable in what it has to offer. I hope people look at it. I hope they begin to feel some of the experiences that this family has felt, and others. And I hope more understanding comes out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group picture-- the very few pictures that I have of my family. And sitting from left to right is my Aunt Broncia, my grandfather Moishe, my Grandma Rachel, my mother, and my sister Chana. Standing from left to right-- my feter-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, what's his name? Mendel, my feter Mendel, my feter Shmuel. Those were two brothers of my mother. And next to him is my father, Avrom Minsky. And the picture that's not too clear is my sister Mala.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, Mala looks like she was five or six years. It would be in the 1920s. It was before I was born. So I assume that would be about 1925, '24.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, in my grandfather's and grandmother's house. And it looks like it was some kind of celebration, either yontif or Shabbos. [SIGHS] This is a picture of my mother and my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those pictures were both taken in Lodz. My mother's picture would have been taken [INAUDIBLE] sometimes in the 1930s-- either '34 or '35. My father's picture would have been about 1930 or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Nacha Grundman Minsky. And my father's name was Avrom Minsky. This is my mother's youngest brother, [? Yisrael ?] Baruch Grundman. [SOBBING] He was my favorite uncle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Lodz in the 1930s. And because of his picture, we have a picture of my brother Laibele, because it was found [SOBBING] in the garbage. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He died about 1941-- we didn't know exactly-- [SOBBING] in the partisans. And it's a long story with him. Maybe some day I will write about him. This is picture after liberation. And this is my husband, Moishe Senderowicz-- or now Morris Sender-- and I in the DP camps in about 1947. And here, we look like human beings already. This picture was taking in-- taken in 1946 in the DP camp in Pocking, Germany. And I was pregnant with my first son. And to us, it was a miracle. So that's why I say it's a miracle baby. Because we were worried that we won't have any children. This picture is Morris, or Moishe, or Moniek-- I call him Moniek-- his Yiddish name is Moishe. And now his official English name is Morris-- and our first newborn baby, one-- one of the very few new babies in the DP camp in Leipheim. It was a barrack that became a hospital. And he was delivered by a midwife that considered each child she delivered a miracle child. So it's a very special picture. And obviously, we are very happy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1946. August 1946. This picture was taking in Leipheim, the DP camp Leipheim in Germany, in 1947. From left to right, sitting, is my sister Chana. The little boy on my sister [? Molly's ?] lap-- that's his mother. And this is Avrom, or Abe we call him now. Next to him is-- am I, holding my son Laibele-- or Louis, now. Next to her is my sister-in-law, [? Edra. ?] And standing, from left to right, is my brother-in-law [? Moniek ?] Weintraub. Next to him is my late brother-in-law [? Yosef ?] [? Labelski. ?] Next to him is my husband, Moniek. And next to him is my brother Yankl Minsky. This picture was taking in 1949, DP camp [? Leffeld, ?] Germany. Standing is the proud brother Laibele, then Senderowicz, now Louis Sender. And in the carriage is his little brother, Avrom Senderowicz, now Allen Sender. And this was the second child that I had in the DP camps. This picture is already in the United States. And standing, from left to right again, is Louis. Sitting next to the carriage is Allen-- now that we're called Sender-- and Harvey, who was born in the United States. And standing is my husband, Moniek, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1951. End of 1951. This is-- this was taken August 1994 in Denver, Colorado. We were celebrating one of the grandchildren's bar mitzvah, Ben, standing in back of me. And that was his bar mitzvah. And that's one occasion that we are all together. Sitting in the front, on the bottom, is Allen Sender, my son, in back of-- well, we go across, or back of him. In back of him is Mindy, his wife, and their sons. There's Eric, standing back of Mindy. Next to Mindy is Ben and Matthew. This is that group. And next to them, standing, is my daughter, Nancy. Next to Nancy is Harvey, whose son's bar mitzvah we're celebrating here. Ben is next to him, below. And then is Joanne, his wife, next to Ben. Sitting to the front is Morris and I. And standing next to Morris is Katie. In back of Katie, standing, is Amy. Next to her is Jennifer. And then is Judy, my daughter-in-law. Jennifer and Amy are grandchildren. And sitting down is Louis, my son. And they're all Senders, so I didn't give last names. [LAUGHS] It seems like I got my grandsons mixed up. [LAUGHS] Standing is Brian, the top line. And below him is his little brother, Matthew. And Ben is a little bit further, if you move the camera a little bit. Not this way, the other way. Keep going, keep going, keep going. That's Ben. That's his bar mitzvah. Now I think we got them all right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said in the interview, I see-- survived because of poetry and a vicious Nazi guard. But this is the poetry that I had written after she gave me a booklet. I rewrote it from the little paper-- scraps of paper. And on the top there, it says, 1944 and whatever date is there. I can't see from here what the date is. One of the poems that I would like to read-- it's in the middle there. This was the top poem there-- there. [YIDDISH], which means my friend, because I turned-- those little scraps of paper were my friend. And I turned to them to put my feelings-- to share my feelings with them. One of the poems that I wrote-- it's called 'Why.' Should I read it Yiddish and English? In Yiddish, it's [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] [YIDDISH] means 'Why.' Written by Riva Minska, November-- number 55082, Camp Mittelsteine, Germany. January 14, 1945. Number 55082 was my number. Translated from Yiddish by Ruth Minsky Senderowicz, free person, New York City, USA, 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'All alone, I stare out the window feeling my soul in me cry, hearing the painful screams of my heart calling silently, why? Why are your dreams scattered, destroyed? Why are you put in this cage? Why is the world silently watching? Why can't they hear your rage? Why is the barbed wire holding me prisoner, blocking to freedom my way? Why do I still keep waiting and dreaming, hoping? Maybe someday I see above me snow-covered mountains, majestic, proud, and high. If, like a free bird, I could reach their peaks, maybe from there the world would hear my cry, why?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Some students in California, after hearing me lecture there-- I was a week there-- and reading my books, decided to put themself in my place. And they took brown paper bags, crumpled them up, burned the edges, and they became the people in the book. And this was sent to me about two years ago, in 1994. It's very touching. Because if you go over each page, which we don't do now, they really expressed a lot of the emotions that I felt at that time. Some of them put themself in the cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was presented to me in 1995, May 1995, by students of Hauppauge Middle School. I come there every year. And every year, besides flowers, they have birthday cakes and many different presents for me. We have a date already for next year, May 1. Now, this was sent to me on my birthday, May 3rd, 1996, from a Plainview Middle School, where I had lectured a few weeks before that. They had put together 130-- 130 wonderful letters and sent it Federal Express to make sure that it gets there in time of my birthday. And it was a very touching birthday present. This was embroidered by a mother of a student in California that I don't know the student. I don't know the mother. But she had read The kids had an assignment in school. But she made it her own assignment. And she was so touched that she embroidered it and send it to me. And I keep it on the refrigerator. Because it says, when hope is lost, all is lost. In this there's the message, as long as there's life, there is hope-- a different version-- that I held on to through all those tough years. And it's my mother's legacy. Those are the books that I have written, They're each about my experiences, beginning from the ghetto of Lodz, till Auschwitz, to Mittelsteine, to Grafenort, and the DP camps. And is the importance of the teaching the Holocaust and the effect I had on the children and on the grandchildren of survivors, trying to live with the past and also live with the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: The green boxes [BACKROUND NOISE] here are And the next one is Those are talking books for the handicapped. They need a special machine to play it. But they-- they're usually available to the libraries. And the other books are translations in different languages. Also there is one from Denmark that's a talking book. And there is translation in Dutch, in Danish, in the [INAUDIBLE] Islands, which I don't know what language. They-- they speaking, but of course, I can't read the translation. The books are used in Canada, which is English. What else is there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and I are both children that weren't supposed to survive. And we are here. We have children and grandchildren. And we celebrated our 50th anniversary July 7, 1995. And this picture is of a cruise that was given to us by our children to enjoy 50 years of being together and surviving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Sender, for sharing this experience with us today.
